before.title: Frances Oldham Kelsey Awarded for Thalidomide Refusal
before.one_liner: Frances Oldham Kelsey is awarded the U.S. President's Award for refusing to authorize thalidomide

after.title: Frances Oldham Kelsey Awarded for Thalidomide Refusal
after.one_liner: Frances Oldham Kelsey receives a presidential service award after refusing to authorize thalidomide without stronger safety evidence
after.description: Canadian-born American pharmacologist Frances Oldham Kelsey receives the President's Award for Distinguished Federal Civilian Service after refusing to authorize thalidomide without stronger safety evidence.

after.summary: Canadian-born American pharmacologist Frances Oldham Kelsey receives the President's Award for Distinguished Federal Civilian Service after refusing to authorize thalidomide without stronger safety evidence. Her caution helped keep a drug later linked to severe birth defects from being approved in the United States.

after.deep_dive: Canadian-born American pharmacologist Frances Oldham Kelsey receives the President's Award for Distinguished Federal Civilian Service after refusing to authorize thalidomide without stronger safety evidence. Her caution helped keep a drug later linked to severe birth defects from being approved in the United States.
after.why_it_matters: Kelsey’s review helped strengthen public trust in drug safety by showing that regulatory caution can prevent irreversible harm.

before.title: Woodrow Wilson's Stroke at the White House
before.one_liner: U.S. President Woodrow Wilson suffers a catastrophic stroke at the White House, leaving him incapacitated for the remainder of his presidency

before.title: Night of the Pencils
before.one_liner: A group of high-school students in La Plata, Argentina, were kidnapped, tortured, raped, and murdered during the Argentinian military dictatorship

after.title: Night of the Pencils
after.one_liner: Security forces abduct high-school students in La Plata, Argentina, during the military dictatorship
after.description: Security forces in La Plata, Argentina abduct high-school students on September 16 and 17, 1976, in what becomes known as the Night of the Pencils.
after.short_summary: Security forces abduct high-school students in La Plata, Argentina, during the military dictatorship
after.summary: Security forces in La Plata, Argentina abduct high-school students on September 16 and 17, 1976, in what becomes known as the Night of the Pencils. The students are taken to clandestine detention centers; reporting describes imprisonment, torture, and deaths, with six never seen again.
after.detail: Security forces in La Plata, Argentina abduct high-school students on September 16 and 17, 1976, in what becomes known as the Night of the Pencils. The students are taken to clandestine detention centers; reporting describes imprisonment, torture, and deaths, with six never seen again.
after.deep_dive: Security forces in La Plata, Argentina abduct high-school students on September 16 and 17, 1976, in what becomes known as the Night of the Pencils. The students are taken to clandestine detention centers; reporting describes imprisonment, torture, and deaths, with six never seen again.
after.why_it_matters: The case remains a stark symbol of Argentina’s dictatorship-era repression and the vulnerability of student activists under state terror.

before.title: Emmett Till Murder Trial
before.one_liner: An all-white jury in Mississippi acquits Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam in the murder of Emmett Till

after.title: Emmett Till Murder Trial
after.one_liner: An all-white Mississippi jury acquits J. W. Milam and Roy Bryant of murdering Emmett Till

after.short_summary: An all-white Mississippi jury acquits J. W. Milam and Roy Bryant of murdering Emmett Till
